html:
For a biography of Anne Charlotte Leffler about Kovalevskaya, see Sonja Kovalevsky (biography).

Sofia Vasiljevna Kovalevskaya (Софья Васильевна Ковалевская), born Krjukovskaja on January 15, 1850, in Moscow, Russia, and died on February 10, 1891, in Stockholm, was a Russian mathematician who made remarkable contributions to mathematical analysis, partial differential equations, and mechanics. She was also a fiction writer, and she sometimes used the surname Korvin-Krukovskaja, but worked under the pen name Sonja Kovalevsky. She lived in Sweden from 1881, where three years later she was appointed professor, becoming the first female professor in Europe. She was a pioneer for women in mathematics around the world. In addition to being the first female professor, she was also the first woman to earn a doctorate in mathematics and one of the first women to work as an editor for a scientific journal. According to science historian Ann Hibner Koblitz, Kovalevskaya was "the most famous female scientist before the twentieth century."

== Biography == Kovalevskaya's father was Vasilij Vasiljevitj Krjukovskij (1800–1874), an officer in the Russian artillery, later a general of Polish origin. He encouraged Sofia's early interest in mathematics and natural sciences, and she studied mathematics at several universities in Germany: Heidelberg, Berlin, and Göttingen. Despite her mathematical talent, she could not complete her education in Russia because women were not allowed to study at universities there. To gain permission to study abroad, she entered a marriage of convenience with the paleontologist Vladimir Kovalevskij in 1868, which was dissolved in 1883 by his suicide.

She studied partly in Göttingen but especially in Berlin under Karl Weierstrass, who had to teach her privately because women were not allowed to attend public mathematics lectures. He was very impressed with her mathematical knowledge and taught her for three years, covering the same material as his university lectures. In 1874, Kovalevskaya presented three papers – on partial differential equations, on the dynamics of Saturn's rings, and on elliptic integrals – as her doctoral dissertation at the University of Göttingen. Sofia Kovalevskaya thus obtained her doctorate, with Weierstrass managing to exempt her from the oral examination. She became the first woman to earn a doctorate (in the modern sense) in mathematics. Her paper on partial differential equations contains what is now known as the Cauchy-Kovalevskaja theorem, which proves the existence and analyticity of local solutions to equations defined under appropriate boundary conditions.

She moved to Sweden in 1881, and thanks to her great talent, she was invited to Stockholm University for a teaching position by Gösta Mittag-Leffler, where she worked from 1883. In 1884, Mittag-Leffler managed to secure a professorship for her at Stockholm University, making her the first female professor in Sweden and the second female mathematics professor in the world, after Maria Gaetana Agnesi (who never took up her professorship). It should be noted that Stockholm University was private at this time, and her appointment did not represent a breakthrough for women's opportunities to hold positions on equal terms with men at state universities. She worked, among other things, on partial differential equations. August Strindberg wrote in an article that Sonja Kovalevsky had based her papers on Weierstrass's work and that she was not a great mathematician, which was completely unfounded.

In 1888, she won the prestigious Prix Bordin, awarded by the French Academy of Sciences, for her mathematical paper "On the Rotation of a Solid Body Around a Fixed Point," which was considered to be of such high quality that she was awarded double the prize money.

In 1885, she was nominated for membership in the Royal Swedish Academy of Sciences, but several members opposed this because she was a woman. The permanent secretary, Georg Lindhagen, argued that this would go against the statutes, which clearly stated "man" (although there had been exceptions since Eva Ekeblad was elected in 1748). In 1887, she was elected to the newly formed Society De 17, where Gösta Mittag-Leffler participated as one of the founders and was also the society's first secretary. Together with Anne Charlotte Leffler, sister of Gösta Mittag-Leffler, Kovalevskaya was also elected to Nya Idun in 1885.

She was also active in literature. In 1887, she wrote the drama "Kampen för lyckan" (The Struggle for Happiness) together with Anne Charlotte Leffler, and in 1889, her autobiographical novel "Ur ryska livet: Systrarna Rajevski" (From Russian Life: The Raevsky Sisters) was published. In 1892, "Sonja Kovalevsky: Hvad jag har upplefvat tillsammans med henne och hvad hon berättat mig om sig själf" (Sofia Kovalevskaya: What I Have Experienced with Her and What She Told Me About Herself) was published, a biography of Kovalevskaya written by her friend Anne Charlotte Leffler.

She died in Stockholm in 1891 from influenza (during the ongoing pandemic), which was exacerbated by lung

How certain can we be about this?

Information about individuals from the 1800s is very well documented (through census records, newspapers, and letters). The uncertainty primarily concerns the selection of data and perspective – whose stories are being highlighted.
tldr: Sofia Kovalevskaya was a Russian mathematician who became the first woman in Europe to earn the title of professor. She was a pioneer for women in science and made significant discoveries in mathematics.
lattlast:
Sofia Kovalevskaya was born in Russia in 1850 and moved to Sweden in 1881. She became a highly talented mathematician, despite the difficulties women faced in studying at universities during that time. By studying abroad, she managed to earn her degree and became the first woman in Europe to receive the title of professor of mathematics.

She worked at Stockholm University and won several important awards for her mathematical calculations. She was a pioneer, meaning she opened doors for other women in science. In addition to mathematics, she also wrote books and plays.

Sofia died in Stockholm in 1891 after contracting influenza. Today, she is remembered as one of the most important female scientists of her time.
